content.problemStatement:
There are NNN cars (numbered 111 through NNN) on a circular track with length NNN. For each iii (2≤i≤N2 \le i \le N2≤i≤N), the iii-th of them is at a distance i−1i-1i−1 clockwise from car 111, i.e. car 111 needs to travel a distance i−1i-1i−1 clockwise to reach car iii. Also, for each valid iii, the iii-th car has fif_ifi​ litres of gasoline in it initially.

You are driving car 111 in the clockwise direction. To move one unit of distance in this direction, you need to spend 111 litre of gasoline. When you pass another car (even if you'd run out of gasoline exactly at that point), you steal all its gasoline. Once you do not have any gasoline left, you stop.

What is the total clockwise distance travelled by your car?
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers f1,f2,…,fNf_1, f_2, \ldots, f_Nf1​,f2​,…,fN​.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the total clockwise distance travelled.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The car starts with 333 liters of gasoline. Using this, the car can travel 333 units. On its journey, there is no other car having gasoline. Thus, the car stops after 333 units.
Test case 222: The car starts with 111 liter of gasoline.

After traveling 111 unit, it passes another car with 111 liter gasoline. Thus, the amount of gasoline left after traveling 111 unit is 111 liter.
After traveling 222 units, it passes another car with 111 liter gasoline. Thus, the amount of gasoline left after traveling 222 units is 111 liter.
After traveling 333 units, it passes another car with 111 liter gasoline. Thus, the amount of gasoline left after traveling 333 units is 111 liter.
After traveling 444 units, it passes another car with 111 liter gasoline. Thus, the amount of gasoline left after traveling 444 units is 111 liter.
After traveling 555 units, there is no car with gasoline. Thus, the car stops after 555 units.

Test case 333: The car starts with 555 liters of gasoline.

After traveling 111 unit, it passes another car with 444 liters gasoline. Thus, the amount of gasoline left after traveling 111 unit is 5−1+4=85-1+4 = 85−1+4=8 liters.
After traveling 222 units, it passes another car with 333 liters gasoline. Thus, the amount of gasoline left after traveling 222 units is 8−1+3=108-1+3 = 108−1+3=10 liters.
After traveling 333 units, it passes another car with 222 liters gasoline. Thus, the amount of gasoline left after traveling 333 units is 10−1+2=1110-1+2 = 1110−1+2=11 liters.
After traveling 444 units, it passes another car with 111 liter gasoline. Thus, the amount of gasoline left after traveling 444 units is 11−1+1=1111-1+1 = 1111−1+1=11 liters.
After traveling 555 units, there is no car with gasoline. Thus, after 555 units, 101010 liters of gasoline is left.

The car can move 101010 more units using the leftover gasoline. The total distance travelled is 151515 units.
